segments.s542-g81: نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression: نگه (gaze) + کرد (past 3sg of کردن) = “he looked / he fixed his gaze.” جایی is the noun “place” and که introduces a relative clause “where…”. بد خارستان is a noun phrase (“bad/harsh thorn-thicket”) placed after که as the head of that relative clause; word order is somewhat compressed and poetic (verb before the clause-final descriptive phrase).
segments.s542-g82: ازو is the prepositional pronoun “from it / out of it.” کرد here is again past 3sg of کردن meaning “he made/caused.” خرم is an adjective (‘prosperous / lush / joyful’) modifying شارستان; یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective “one / a certain,” and شارستان is the noun “town/city.” The clause means literally “from it he made a prosperous one town.”
segments.s542-g83: کجا is the interrogative “where.” گندشاپور is a proper name (Gondeshapur). خواندی is past 2nd singular of خواندن used here in the sense “you called/named” (older usage); ورا is the unstressed object pronoun “it” placed after the verb, so خواندی ورا = “you called it.”
segments.s542-g84: جزین = “except this / apart from this” introducing the exception. نام نامی is a tautological NP: نام “name” + نامی “famous,” i.e. “a famous name.” نراندی is a past form with negative prefix ن- here meaning “you did not (bestow/give)” in this context (archaic/poetic use), and ورا again is the object pronoun “to it / on it.” Together: “Except for this name, you did not give it a famous name.”
segments.s542-g33: ز پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from/in front of his father' (ز + پیش + noun). گوی is the direct object (here 'the orb' or a symbolic spherical object) and بربود is a simple past (poetic) verb meaning 'carried off / took away'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', followed by برد in the past as a second verb echoing the action ('bore off/took'). Word order is typical for older/poetic Persian: prepositional phrase first, then object and past verbs.
segments.s542-g34: چو شد دور مر is a temporal clause 'when he became distant from me' — چو 'when', شد past/auxiliary 'became', دور 'distant', مر = 'to/for me' (archaic form of me). کودکان را سپرد is the main clause following it: کودکان 'the children' + را (definite object marker) and سپرد past tense 'entrusted/handed over'. The pronoun 'مر' marks the perspective of 'me' in the preceding clause.
segments.s542-g35: ز شادی is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/with joy'. چنان شد is a fixed expression 'became so' (چنان = 'so/thus', شد = past 'became'). دل اردشیر is the subject phrase 'Ardashir's heart' (دل follows the verb in poetic inversion). Overall: 'Ardashir's heart became thus from joy.'
segments.s542-g36: که introduces a result/relative clause 'that/so that'. گردد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to become' used here to express the resulting state. جوان مردم is a noun phrase 'the young of the people' or 'the youths of the people'. گشته پیر is a perfect/participle phrase 'having become old' (گشته = perfect participle 'become/turned', پیر = 'old'), so the clause means 'that the young of the people were turned old.'
segments.s542-g37: سوارانش is the subject 'his horsemen/riders' (ـش suffix = 'his'). از خاک برداشتند is a past transitive clause 'they lifted/took (him) from the dust/ground' (از = 'from', خاک = 'dust/earth', برداشتند = past 'they took/removed/raised'). The verb is plural, agreeing with سوارانش.
segments.s542-g38: همی is a habitual/continuative adverb here ('continually' or emphatic), and دست بر دست بگذاشتند literally 'they put hand upon hand' — an idiomatic description of clasping hands or layering hands (bگذاشتند past 'they placed'). The sequence expresses repeated or simultaneous action in past narrative.
segments.s542-g39: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor'. زان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/then' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). گرفتش is the past verb with enclitic object pronoun 'he took him/received him' (گرفت + ش). به بر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to his bosom' or 'into his embrace' (به + بر = to/upon the chest).
segments.s542-g40: همی here is emphatic ('indeed/continually'), آفرین is an exclamation/noun meaning 'praise' or 'bravo', and خواند is the past verb 'he uttered/voiced/said' (خواند = 'read/recited/voiced'). بر دادگر is the prepositional phrase 'to the just one' (بر used as 'to/toward' here, دادگر = 'the just ruler' or 'just man'). The whole means he repeatedly voiced praise toward the just one.
segments.s542-g57: ببخشید is a literary past 3rd‑person verb (بخشیدن) meaning 'he bestowed/gave' here rather than 'forgive'; it appears first (verb‑initial narrative style). چندان is an adverb 'so much/that much' modifying the extent of the giving, ورا is the indirect object pronoun 'to her', and خواسته is a participial/nominal form 'desired' (i.e. 'that she desired'), so the phrase means 'he gave to her as much as she desired.'
segments.s542-g58: که introduces a result/relative clause ('so that/that'), شد is past 3rd‑person 'became' (شدن), and کاخ و ایوانش is the noun phrase 'the palace and its iwan' (ایوانش = iwan + possessive suffix -ش 'his/her/its'). آراسته is a past‑participle/adjectival predicate 'adorned'; together: 'that the palace and its iwan became adorned.'
segments.s542-g59: بفرمود is a polite/literary past 3rd‑person verb meaning 'he commanded/said'; تا here introduces a purpose/result subordinate clause ('so that/that'), and دختر اردوان is the noun phrase 'Ardavan's daughter' serving as the subject of the following subordinate clause.
segments.s542-g60: به + ایوان is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the iwan'; شود is the present/subjunctive or jussive form of شدن used in the subordinate clause introduced by تا ('become / be brought / be made to be'), and شاد و روشن‌روان are predicate adjectives 'happy/joyful and bright‑soulèd/cheerful' describing the daughter. Word order places the location phrase before the verb, and the adjectives follow the verb as its predicative complement.
segments.s542-g61: ببخشید here is past 3rd‑person of بخشیدن with the sense 'he forgave/pardoned' (not 'gave'); کرده + گناه is a participial construction meaning 'the sin (that) she had committed' (کرده is the perfect participle 'done'), and ورا is the indirect object pronoun 'for her' — together: 'he pardoned the sin she had done for her.'
segments.s542-g62: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', زنگار 'tarnish/stain' is the direct object, and بزدود is past 3rd‑person 'he removed/wiped away' (بزدودن). ماه ورا ('her moon') is a poetic kenning for her face; the order places the object before the verb (typical in classical Persian narrative) and the indirect pronoun after the noun.
segments.s542-g63: بیاورد is the past 3rd‑person form 'he brought' (بیاوردن). فرهنگیان را is the definite direct object ('the men of learning' / 'the learned men') marked by the object marker را, and به شهر is the prepositional phrase 'into/to the city' indicating destination.
segments.s542-g64: کسی is 'someone/anyone'; کو here is the archaic/poetic relative particle (equivalent to که) meaning 'who' (not the interrogative 'where'), z (ز) means 'from' or 'by' and introduces the source/quality, فرزانگی is 'wisdom/learning', داشت is past 'had/possessed', and بهر is a preposition 'for/toward' (often 'for the sake of' or 'for the purpose of'). The whole fragment reads 'anyone who possessed wisdom for (the purpose)...' (the clause is resumed by the following lines).
segments.s542-g25: بود is the copula in past form (was); in classical Persian the verb often precedes its nominal complement. بی‌گمان is an adverb ‘undoubtedly’ modifying the whole clause. پاک فرزند من is a noun phrase with the adjective پاک before فرزند (poetic word order) and the possessor من following the noun as in Persian ('my son').
segments.s542-g26: ز is the archaic/pre‑poetic preposition meaning ‘from/of’. تخم و بر are coordinated nouns (literary pair meaning ‘seed and stock/line’), connected by و; پاک qualifies پیوند (pure lineage). پیوند من is a genitive construction ‘the bond/lineage of mine’ with the possessor after the head noun. The whole phrase gives the origin/lineage of the son.
segments.s542-g27: به فرمان is an adverbial prepositional phrase ‘at/by the command’. بشد is a classical/periphrastic past-form meaning essentially ‘it happened / he complied’ (verb before subject is normal in poetic diction). بنده شهریار is the agent (the king’s servant), with noun بنده followed by its modifier شهریار.
segments.s542-g28: بزد is perfective/past ‘he struck’; گوی is the (omitted‑ra) direct object ‘the ball/sphere’ (classical Persian often omits the object marker را). و links the two verbs; افگند is another past verb ‘he threw/cast (it)’ and پیش سوار is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in front of the rider’ (پیش = before/in the presence of).
segments.s542-g29: دوان is an adjectival/participial form ‘running/rushing’ modifying کودکان (word order is modifier + noun). از پی او is a fixed phrase ‘after him / in pursuit of him’ (از + پی = after), and چو تیر is a simile ‘like an arrow’ (چو = like).
segments.s542-g30: چو at the clause head is the subordinating particle ‘when/as’. گشتند is past plural ‘they became/turned/arrived’ (verb after the particle), نزدیک is an adverb/adjective ‘near’, با here means ‘to/with’ indicating proximity toward someone, and اردشیر is the proper name they approach — together ‘when they drew near to Ardashir’.
segments.s542-g31: بماندند is past plural ‘they remained/were left’. ناکام is a post‑positive adjective ‘unsuccessful/thwarted’. بر جای خویش is a locative phrase ‘in their own place’ (بر = on/in, جای = place, خویش + ش = ‘own’ with the enclitic possessive), so the whole segment means they stayed, unsuccessful, in their places.
segments.s542-g32: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. شاپور is the subject name. گرد here functions adverbially ‘around/turned (about)’ and اندر آمد is a compound verb ‘came in/entered / came forward’ (اندر آمد = came). به پیش is a direction/purpose phrase ‘to the presence of / before (someone)’. Altogether: ‘when Shapur turned and came forward.’
segments.s542-g1: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came/arrived' placed at the start (poetic inversion). به شبگیر is a prepositional/time phrase ('at nightfall' or 'toward night') modifying the verb, and دستور شاه ('the king's order/command') is the noun phrase that the verb takes — the clause literally reads 'came at nightfall the king's command.'
segments.s542-g2: همی کرد is a 3rd-person past verb complex where همی gives an archaic/emphatic or progressive nuance and کرد is 'did/ordered'; the subject (the king) is omitted but carried over from the previous clause. کودک is the (unstated-marked) object and به میدان سپاه is a directional prepositional phrase 'to the army's field/ground.'
segments.s542-g3: This is a verbless (elliptical) poetic listing: یکی ... یکی frames likeness — 'one (person) — garment and face and bearing (were) the same.' The final یکی repeats for emphasis; the copula ('was') is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s542-g4: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. پیدا نبد is an archaic negative copula construction ('did not appear' / 'was not visible' — نبد = نبـود). این ازان اندکی literally 'this among them little' = 'such a thing was little/rare among them,' with inverted word order (noun phrase + quantifier at the end).
segments.s542-g5: به میدان gives the locale ('in/at the field'); تو is dative/indirect object 'to you'; گفتی here functions impersonally/communicatively ('it was said/told to you' or 'you were told') rather than a literal 'you said' (a classical idiom). یکی سور بود is a subordinate content clause 'that there was a banquet' (یکی = 'a certain/one', سور = 'feast', بود = past copula 'was').
segments.s542-g6: میان اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the middle of the inner (quarters)'; شاه شاپور is the full subject (the king, named Shapur) and بود is the past copula 'was' — straightforward S(pb) predicate order typical in poetry.
segments.s542-g7: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' کودک is the subject 'the child.' به زخم اندر آورد گوی is a verb phrase 'brought word about the wound' — اندر آورد is a classical compound verb 'brought/related,' and گوی here is the noun 'saying/word/report' (brought news of the wound).
segments.s542-g8: فزونی همی جست is a poetic phrase where فزونی ('increase/greater commotion') + همی (archaic emphatic/progressive) + جست (leapt/sprang up) yields 'a greater commotion sprang up.' هر یک is 'each one,' and بدوی — here in context means roughly 'ran/hurried to (it/him)' (an archaic/contracted form); the last word is an irregular/archaic verbal form rather than the adjective sense found in the lemma list.
segments.s542-g9: بیامد is a past tense 3rd-person singular verb 'came' placed before the rest (verb-first, a common classical/poetic order). به میدان پگاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the field at dawn' (به = to/into; میدان = field/arena; پگاه = early morning/dawn). اردشیر is the named subject placed after the temporal/prepositional phrase, so the whole segment reads 'Ardashir came to the field at dawn.'
segments.s542-g10: تنی چند از ویژگان ناگزیر is a noun phrase appositive/continuation describing companions: تنی چند = 'a few (persons)'; از ویژگان = 'of the distinguished/elite'; ناگزیر here is an adjective meaning 'compelled/forced' (not merely 'inevitable'), so the phrase means 'a few of the distinguished, compelled (to come).' The verb implied (came) links back to بیامد in the previous segment.
segments.s542-g11: نگه کرد is a compound: نگه (gaze/watch) plus کرد ('did') to form 'he looked/kept watch.' و چون ... بدید links the next action: و = 'and', چون here functions as a subordinating conjunction ('when' in context, though one listed sense is 'like/as'), and کودکان را بدید = 'he saw the children' (را is the definite object marker). The sequence gives 'he looked, and when he saw the children...'.
segments.s542-g12: این عبارت is a simple past action: یکی باد سرد = 'a (sudden) cold wind/gust' (یکی here = 'a certain' or 'one'), از جگر = 'from the jigar' (jigar = seat of feeling/heart in poetry), and برکشید is past 'rose/was lifted' (literally 'lifted up'). So the clause means 'a cold gust rose from his heart.'
segments.s542-g13: به انگشت بنمود is an instrumental/gestural construction: به + انگشت here indicates the motion 'by/with his finger' and بنمود (past of نمودن) = 'he showed/indicated/pointed.' با کدخدای = 'to/with the village headman' (here 'he signalled to the headman'). Word order places the instrument before the verb (poetic flexibility).
segments.s542-g14: که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'who/that'; آمد = 'came'; یکی اردشیری = 'a certain Ardashiri' (یکی = a certain/one, اردشیری = proper name or adjectival form); به جای = a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in place (of)/instead' (به + جای). Together: 'who came — a certain Ardashiri — in his place.'
segments.s542-g15: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (بدو راهبر گفت = 'the guide said to him'); راهبر is the speaker 'the guide/leader'; گفت is the past 'said' (here used as the reporting verb); کای پادشا is a vocative 'O king' addressing the ruler. Word order puts the indirect object pronoun first (classical style).
segments.s542-g16: دلت = دل + -ت (your heart) with شد 'became' giving 'your heart has become/turned'; به فرزند خود = 'toward/to your son (own son)' (خود = reflexive 'own'); بر گواه is a short phrase meaning 'as/for a witness' (بر = on/upon, گواه = witness), so the whole clause means 'your heart has turned toward your own son as witness' (i.e., you favor or rely on your son as a witness).
segments.s542-g17: This is a inverted, somewhat archaic word order: one (یکی) + servant (بنده) + object marker (را) + said (گفت) + subject 'king Ardashir' (شاه اردشیر). Put plainly, ‘شاه اردشیر گفت به یکی بنده’ — 'Ardashir the king said to a certain servant.' 'را' marks بنده as the definite/targeted person of the speech.
segments.s542-g18: که introduces the content of the king's words ('that' / 'saying that'). رو here functions adverbially (‘toward/forward’ or archaic locative) linked to گوی ('ball'); ایشان = 'their/them' (referring to the others on the field); به چوگان بگیر is an imperative clause, with بگیر (imperative 'take') governing how: به چوگان = 'with the mallet'. The whole means roughly '...that (you) move/take their ball with the mallet.'
segments.s542-g19: همی باش is an emphatic imperative: همی (continued/ever) + باش (be/remain), so 'keep yourself/ stay'. با کودکان تازه‌روی is a prepositional phrase 'with the fresh-faced children' (تازه‌روی = 'new-faced' / 'fresh-faced', i.e. young players).
segments.s542-g20: This is a command about how to move the ball: به چوگان = 'by/with the mallet' (به used adverbially: 'by means of'), به پیش من = 'toward me/in front of me' (پیش = in the presence of/toward), انداز is the imperative stem of اندازیدن ('throw/send'), and گوی 'the ball' is the direct object. Together: 'send/throw the ball to me with the mallet.'
segments.s542-g21: ازان کودکان = 'from/among those children' (ازان = poetic 'from those'); تا که آید = 'until there comes' (تا que introduces the temporal clause); دلیر = 'a brave one' (predicate of the subordinate clause). So: 'from those children, until a brave (one) comes...'.
segments.s542-g22: میان سواران = 'among/amid the horsemen' (میان = among); به کردار شیر uses به + noun to mean 'in the manner/character of' (کردار = conduct/behavior, شیر = lion), i.e. 'acting like a lion.' This qualifies the brave man mentioned before.
segments.s542-g23: ز (poetic variant of از) دیدار من = 'from my sight' or 'out of my view' (دیدار = sight/seeing; من = me). گوی بیرون برد is a verb phrase: گوی (the ball) + بیرون (out/away) + برد (took/carried off) — literally '(he) carried the ball out of sight.'
segments.s542-g24: ازین انجمن = 'from this assembly' (از + این); کس به کس نشمرد = 'he did not count/ reckon one person to another' — more simply 'he did not count anyone in this assembly.' نشمرد is the negative past of شمردن (to count/reckon). The clause states that no one of that gathering was reckoned (by him).
segments.s542-g73: به یک روی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on one side/one face' (the preposition به precedes the noun). بد نام functions as a compound adjective 'ill‑reputed/ill‑famed' modifying شاه اردشیر; Persian often places such adjectives before the noun and omits an explicit copula (was/is).
segments.s542-g74: به روی دگر = 'on the other side' (another prepositional phrase). نام فرخ وزیر is a nominative phrase 'the name (i.e. the man) Farrokh, the vizier' — Persian here omits the copula, so 'نام' ('name') introduces the proper name/title that follows.
segments.s542-g75: This string stacks adjectives before the noun: گران 'precious/expensive', خوار 'despised/worthless', بد نام 'ill‑famed' all modify دستور شاه ('the king's decree' — دستور + شاه is a genitive/possessive construction). The adjectives are attributive and placed before the head noun as common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s542-g76: جهاندیده مردی is a noun phrase 'a worldly‑wise man' (جهاندیده = experienced/worldly), and نماینده راه is an appositive/continuation meaning 'a representative/emissary of the road' (راه here = road/path, i.e. an envoy traveling). No copula is expressed; the two nominal phrases are presented in sequence.
segments.s542-g77: نوشتند is the 3rd‑person plural past tense 'they wrote'. بر نامه‌ها ('on/upon the letters') is the oblique object of نوشتند; هم‌چنین ('likewise/thus') qualifies the manner. Verb before its oblique complement is common in poetic word order.
segments.s542-g78: بدو داد is a two‑word sequence 'gave to him' (بدو = 'to him', داد = past 'gave'). The following items فرمان و مهر و نگین are the direct objects listed in a series: 'the decree, the seal, and the signet' (مهر here = seal/stamp, نگین here = signet/ring).
segments.s542-g79: ببخشید is a past transitive verb here meaning 'he bestowed/gave' (not 'forgave'). گنجی (treasure) is the direct object and به درویش مرد ('to the dervish man') is the indirect object; the order Verb + Object + to‑phrase is used in this line.
segments.s542-g80: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun (the man/treasure). خوردش is from خوردن 'to eat/consume' with enclitic ش referring back (he/it); نبودی is the negative past copula/auxiliary 'was not' (i.e. 'he had not eaten'). به جز = 'except', and کارکرد here denotes 'by (his) labor/working' — so the clause means 'who had eaten nothing except by his work.'
segments.s542-g65: نوشتن is the verbal noun (masdar) 'writing' functioning as the object. بیاموختش is the past 3rd‑person form of آموختن with the enclitic object pronoun ‑ش ('it'), literally 'he learned it' — here ‘he learned writing’. پهلوی follows and specifies the language/script (Pahlavi) in which he learned to write; in classical style the language-label comes after the verb phrase.
segments.s542-g66: نشست is the past 3rd‑person of نشستن used intransitively as 'he sat / he took his seat / he assumed (a position)'. سرافرازی و خسروی are two abstract nouns joined by و that describe the position or state he assumed — 'honor/glory' and 'kingly/regal status' — the verb from the clause governs these complements.
segments.s542-g67: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same' modifying جنگ (جنگ را = 'that battle' with the object marker را). گرد کرده is a perfect participial phrase ('having turned / having made round') built from گرد کردن; here it combines with عنان ('reins') — گرد کرده عنان = 'having gathered/turned the reins' (i.e., prepared to maneuver/charge). Grammatically, the participial construction modifies the subsequent action.
segments.s542-g68: ز بالا is a prepositional phrase 'from above' (ز = 'from', بالا = 'above'). به دشمن is 'to/against the enemy' (به = 'to/toward', دشمن = 'enemy'). نمودن here is the infinitive/lexical form 'to show / to make appear' used in a poetic word-order where the subject (سنان) follows the verb; thus the clause reads 'Senan showed (himself/it) to the enemy from above' with سنان as the actor (proper name).
segments.s542-g69: ز is the preposition 'from / in respect of' introducing a list of nominal activities. می‌خوردن is the masdar/infinitive 'eating' (here a noun-like activity), و ... و links the items: بخشش ('giving, grant; generosity') and کار بزم — a genitive compound where کار ('work/affair') takes بزم ('banquet/assembly') as its modifier: 'the work/management of the feast.' The whole phrase enumerates spheres of activity or concern.
segments.s542-g70: سپه جستن is an infinitival/nominal phrase: سپه ('army/host') + جستن ('to seek, to muster') — 'to summon/seek the host.' و links this to کوشش روز رزم, where کوشش is 'effort/striving' qualified by روز رزم ('the day of battle' — روز 'day' + رزم 'battle'). The two coordinated noun phrases describe martial actions: raising the army and exertion on battle day.
segments.s542-g71: وزان پس is a transitional adverbial 'and from that (time) afterward'. دگر here means 'again' or 'anew'. کرد is the past of کردن 'made/did'; the construction کرد X Y means 'made X (into) Y' so میخ (object 'nail/peg') is what he made, and درم (a dirham) is the resulting status/value — 'he made the peg a dirham.' This is a verbless predication where کرد supplies the action of conversion.
segments.s542-g72: همان میخ دینار repeats/continues the predication with the demonstrative 'that same peg' and دینار as the predicate noun ('(made) a dinar') — the verb from the previous segment is understood. و هر بیش و کم is a concise idiomatic phrase: هر ('every') distributing over بیش و کم ('more and less' = surplus and deficit), i.e. 'and every excess and deficiency.' Grammatically the quantifier هر scopes over the coordinated adjectives.
segments.s542-g41: A list of body parts (سر، چشم، رویش) followed by the past tense verb ببوسید (he kissed). The pronominal suffix -ش on رویش (= 'his face') scope-wise typically applies to the coordinated items, so it means 'his head, his eyes, and his face.' The clause ends with و گفت, a simple conjunction + past tense verb 'and said,' linking this action to reported speech that follows.
segments.s542-g42: که introduces reported content ('that'). چونین is an emphatic demonstrative 'such' (not merely 'thus'). نشاید is an impersonal/modal classical form meaning 'it is not fitting/one ought not' and it governs the following verb-form; نهفت is the lexical verb meaning 'to be hidden / to hide' (here used in the sense 'to be hidden'). Together نشاید نهفت = 'it ought not be hidden.'
segments.s542-g43: به دل = 'into the heart' (prepositional phrase indicating mental entry). هرگز 'never' intensifies the negation. این یاد is the object 'this thought/memory.' نگذاشتم is the past first-person of گذاشتن with negative prefix (ن-), meaning 'I did not put/ I did not allow' — here 'I never allowed this thought into my heart.'
segments.s542-g44: که introduces the subordinate content clause. شاپور را uses the postposition را to mark the definite/accusative object (here the person who is the theme of the supposition). کشته is the past participle 'killed' used predicatively, and پنداشتم is the past first-person 'I supposed/thought.' The structure is the familiar Persian pattern 'X را [participle] پنداشتم' = 'I supposed X to have been [participle].'
segments.s542-g45: چو = 'when' (temporal). یزدان is the subject 'God.' مرا (here shown tokenized into م + را) is the recipient 'to me' (dative/accusative), شهریاری = 'sovereignty/kingship' is the thing bestowed, and فزود is the past tense (archaic/poetic) of فزودن 'to increase/multiply/bestow.' Word order is subject — indirect object — object — verb, common in poetry.
segments.s542-g46: ز من = 'of/from me' (poetic marker indicating association: 'a memorial of me'). در جهان 'in the world' is the locative phrase. یادگاری is the noun 'memorial/memento' (a lasting remembrance), and فزود again is the past verb 'he increased/granted.' The clause means 'He caused a memorial of me to arise in the world.'
segments.s542-g47: به فرمان او = 'by his command' (agentive/authoritative prepositional phrase). بر نیابی is a poetic verb phrase: نیابی (you will not find/attain) with the prepositional/locative element بر preceding it; گذر 'passage' is the thing not found. The sense: 'By his command you shall find no passage' (i.e., you cannot pass without his leave).
segments.s542-g48: وگر = 'but if' / 'and if' (conditional). برتر = 'higher' / 'more exalted.' آری here is an archaic/poetic copular/indicative sense meaning roughly 'you are' or 'you stand' (not the ordinary interjection 'yes'); classical poetry often uses such forms elliptically. ز خورشید سر = 'than the sun's head' or 'above the sun's summit' (ز = poetic 'than/of/from'). So the conditional reads 'and if you are loftier, you are above the sun's head.'
segments.s542-g49: گهر (object) precedes the verb here — a common poetic word order — followed by خواست (past 3rd singular of خواستن) “wanted/asked for.” از گنج is a prepositional phrase “from the treasure” specifying source. The coordinated و دینار خواست repeats the verb for emphasis: دینار is a second direct object and the second خواست is the same past-tense verb referring to the same implicit subject (he/they).
segments.s542-g50: گرانمایه is an adjective before the noun (poetic placement) meaning “precious/valuable,” modifying یاقوت (ruby). بسیار is a quantifier meaning “many” here (not merely “very”) and appears before the verb; خواست again is past 3rd singular, repeating the earlier verbal claim for rhetorical effect.
segments.s542-g51: برو here is poetic/elliptical: in context it functions like بر او (“upon him”) rather than the literal imperative “go”; it marks the beneficiary/location. زر و گوهر are the direct objects (gold and gems). بسی is an adverb meaning ‘in abundance’/‘plentifully’ modifying ریختند, which is the past 3rd-person plural of ریختن “to pour/scatter.” The overall clause describes attendants (unspecified) pouring wealth onto/for him.
segments.s542-g52: زبر is used in the spatial/‘on-top’ sense here (poetic ‘upon/over’). مشک و عنبر are nouns in a coordinate phrase (musk and amber). بسی again modifies the following verb to indicate abundance. بیختند is a poetic/preserved past-form describing the act of strewing or lavishing (parallel to ریختند); the verb is 3rd-person plural and continues the narrative action.
segments.s542-g53: ز دینار is a causal/agentive prepositional phrase ‘because of/from the dinars.’ شد is the past of شدن used as a copula meaning ‘became’ or ‘came to be.’ تارکش (tār-kash, ‘string-player’/lute-player) is the nominal predicate and ناپدید is the adjectival/predicative complement ‘vanished/gone,’ so the clause means his lute-player vanished as a result of the dinars.
segments.s542-g54: ز گوهر again gives the cause/setting ‘because of the jewels.’ کسی is an indefinite pronoun that, together with the negated verb ندید, yields the sense ‘no one’ (standard Persian uses کسی + negative to mean nobody). چهره او is the object ‘his face,’ and ندید is the past negative ‘did not see.’ Word order places the object before the verb, as is typical in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s542-g55: به دستور is an agentive phrase ‘by/at the order (of him).’ بر here functions as the preposition ‘upon/over’ (indicating the target of the action). نیز ‘also/likewise’ connects this action to the previous ones. گوهر is the direct object (jewels), and فشاند is a past 3rd-person singular of افشاندن ‘to scatter/sprinkle’ — the clause reports that by order jewels were scattered.
segments.s542-g56: به کرسی زر is a locative phrase ‘onto a golden throne’ (کرسی modified by زر: golden). پیکرش (پیکر + ـش) is the object ‘his body/figure’ placed before the verb. برنشاند is the past causative/caused-motion verb ‘set/placed (upon),’ 3rd-person singular, describing that his body/figure was seated/placed on the golden throne.

sentences.s542-s41: He fixed his gaze on a place where, out of a harsh thorn-thicket, he made one prosperous town.
sentences.s542-s42: Where did you call it Gondeshapur? Apart from this name, you gave it no famous name.
sentences.s542-s17: He snatched the orb from before his father and bore it off; when he was far from me he entrusted the children.
sentences.s542-s18: From joy Ardashir's heart became so that the young of the people were turned old.
sentences.s542-s19: His riders lifted him from the dust and continually put hand to hand.
sentences.s542-s20: Then the king took him to his bosom and continuously uttered praise to the just one.
sentences.s542-s29: He bestowed so much upon her as she desired that the palace and its iwan became adorned.
sentences.s542-s30: He ordered that Ardavan's daughter be brought to the iwan, joyful and bright‑soulèd.
sentences.s542-s31: He forgave the sin she had committed and wiped the tarnish from her moon (i.e. cleansed the stain from her face).
sentences.s542-s32: He brought the men of learning into the city — anyone who possessed wisdom for (the purpose).
sentences.s542-s13: He was, without doubt, my pure son from my seed and noble (pure) lineage.
sentences.s542-s14: At the command the king’s servant obeyed; he struck the ball and threw it before the rider.
sentences.s542-s15: The children rushed after him like arrows; when they became near Ardashir...
sentences.s542-s16: They remained unsuccessful in their place; when Shapur turned and came forward before (him)...
sentences.s542-s1: At nightfall the king's command arrived; he at once ordered the child to the army's field.
sentences.s542-s2: Some were alike in robe, face, and bearing, but such sameness among them was rare.
sentences.s542-s3: In the field you were told that there was a banquet; inside the inner quarters, King Shapur was present.
sentences.s542-s4: When the child brought word of the wound, an outcry rose and each one sprang (to run).
sentences.s542-s5: Ardashir came into the field at dawn, accompanied by a few of the distinguished who were compelled.
sentences.s542-s6: He looked, and when he saw the children a cold gust rose from his heart.
sentences.s542-s7: He pointed with his finger to the village headman, and a certain Ardashiri came forward in his place.
sentences.s542-s8: To him the guide said, 'O king, your heart has turned toward your own son as witness.'
sentences.s542-s9: Ardashir the king said to a certain servant, “Go — take their ball with the mallet.”
sentences.s542-s10: Remain with the fresh-faced children; with the mallet, send the ball toward me.
sentences.s542-s11: Keep watch among those children until a brave one comes amid the horsemen, behaving like a lion.
sentences.s542-s12: He took the ball out of my sight; he did not count anyone from this assembly.
sentences.s542-s37: On one side was the ill‑reputed King Ardashir; on the other side, Farrokh the vizier.
sentences.s542-s38: A costly, despised, ill‑named royal decree; a worldly‑wise man, an emissary of the road.
sentences.s542-s39: They wrote likewise on the letters, and to him they gave the decree, the seal, and the signet.
sentences.s542-s40: He bestowed a treasure on the dervish man, who had eaten nothing except by his labor.
sentences.s542-s33: He learned writing in Pahlavi and took his seat as a figure of honor and kingly rank.
sentences.s542-s34: Having gathered the reins of that same battle, Senan showed himself to the enemy from above.
sentences.s542-s35: In matters of eating and giving and the business of the banquet, and in seeking the host (army) and exertion on the day of battle.
sentences.s542-s36: And from that time on he again made the peg a dirham; that same peg (he made) a dinar, and every excess and deficiency.
sentences.s542-s21: He kissed his head, his eyes, and his face, and said that such a wonder ought not to be hidden.
sentences.s542-s22: I never let this thought enter my heart — that I had supposed Shapur to have been killed.
sentences.s542-s23: When God granted me sovereignty, He also bestowed upon me a memorial in the world.
sentences.s542-s24: By his command you will find no passage; but if you are loftier, you stand above the sun's head.
sentences.s542-s25: He demanded jewels from the treasure and dinars; he wanted many precious rubies.
sentences.s542-s26: Upon him they poured out abundant gold and gems; along with (or on top of) musk and amber they lavishly scattered (them).
sentences.s542-s27: Because of the dinars his lute-player disappeared; because of the jewels no one saw his face.
sentences.s542-s28: By his command they also scattered jewels; they set his form upon a golden throne.
